Midterm 2 W26

2026-03-04

Instructions

This is an in-person exam. Your exam must be complete and uploaded to Gradescope by 2:00p.

Answer the following questions and complete the exercises in RMarkdown. Please embed all of your code and push your final work to your repository. Your code must be organized, clean, and run free from errors. Remember, you must remove the # for any included code chunks to run. Be sure to add your name to the author header above.

Your code must knit in order to be considered. If you are stuck and cannot answer a question, then comment out your code and knit the document. You may use your notes, labs, and homework to help you complete this exam. Do not use any other resources- including AI assistance or other students’ work.

Don’t forget to answer any questions that are asked in the prompt! Each question must be coded; it cannot be answered by a sort in a spreadsheet or a written response only.

For all plots you create, a title and clearly labeled axes must be provided.

Be sure to push your completed midterm to your repository and upload the document to Gradescope. Incorrectly uploading the exam to Gradescope will result in a 5 point deduction. This exam is worth 50 points.

Load the libraries

library(tidyverse)
library(janitor)
library(naniar)
library(ggmap)
library(lubridate)
library(shiny)

API Key

Since you will build a map as part of the exam, take a moment to register your API key. You may need to look up your stadia maps API key online (please do that now).

register_stadiamaps("1046fc07-3fad-4086-aabb-2e4e3a91241d", write = FALSE)

About the data

This study is focused on the fasting behavior of polar bears in the Southern Beaufort (SB) and Chukchi seas (CS). The data were collected from 1983-2017 and include measurements of blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and creatinine, which are used to calculate a ratio that indicates whether a bear is fasting or not. The original paper is included in the data folder. It may help to quickly read the abstract before starting the exam.

The data are from: Rode, K.D., Wilson, R.R., Douglas, D.C., Muhlenbruch, V., Atwood, T.C., Regehr, E.V., Richardson, E.S., Pilfold, N.W., Derocher, A.E., Durner, G.M., Stirling, I., Amstrup, S.C., St. Martin, M., Pagano, A.M., and others, 2017, Spring fasting behavior in a marine apex predator provides an index of ecosystem productivity: Global Change Biology, v. 24, no. 1, p. 410–423, https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13933.

Data resources

  • https://data.usgs.gov/datacatalog/data/USGS:ASC141
  • https://www.sciencebase.gov/catalog/item/61a1a9e7d34eb622f69743d2

The variables include:

  • bear_id: A unique number given to each bear.
  • capture_date: The data when the bear was tranquilized and assessed.
  • popn: The population to which the bear belongs.
  • sex: Sex of each bear.
  • age_class: The age class of each bear (A=adult, S=subadult).
  • mating: Whether or not the bear is pursuing a mate.
  • denning: Whether or not the bear denned last year.
  • lat: Latitude where the bear was located.
  • long: Longitude where the bear was located.
  • urea_nitrogen: Concentration of urea nitrogen.
  • creatinine: Concentration of creatinine.

Load the data

Run the code chunk below to load the data, clean the names, and do some tidying.

polar_bears <- 
  read_csv("data/polarBear_serumUreaCreatinine_beaufortChukchi_rode.csv") %>% 
  clean_names() %>% 
  select(-den_method, -no_cubs, -cub_age) %>%
  filter(age_class==c("A", "S")) %>% 
  mutate(sex=toupper(sex), 
         age_class=toupper(age_class))
  1. (2 points) List the variable names and use a summary function to get an idea of the data structure.
names(polar_bears)
##  [1] "bear_id"       "capture_date"  "popn"          "sex"          
##  [5] "age_class"     "mating"        "denning"       "lat"          
##  [9] "long"          "urea_nitrogen" "creatinine"
glimpse(polar_bears)
## Rows: 519
## Columns: 11
## $ bear_id       <dbl> 6398, 6384, 6520, 6559, 20629, 20876, 20354, 21024, 2076…
## $ capture_date  <date> 1985-04-28, 1991-04-13, 1992-04-18, 2000-04-12, 2003-04…
## $ popn          <chr> "SB", "SB", "SB", "SB", "SB", "SB", "SB", "SB", "SB", "S…
## $ sex           <chr> "F", "F", "F", "F", "F", "F", "F", "F", "F", "F", "F", "…
## $ age_class     <chr> "A", "A", "A", "A", "A", "A", "A", "A", "A", "A", "A", "…
## $ mating        <chr> "no", "no", "no", "no", "no", "no", "no", "no", "no", "n…
## $ denning       <chr> "yes", "yes", "yes", "yes", "yes", "yes", "yes", "yes", …
## $ lat           <dbl> 70.3833, 70.0167, 70.9000, 69.9600, 70.4916, 71.1445, 70…
## $ long          <dbl> -147.6333, -149.5000, -145.2333, -141.8174, -144.9511, -…
## $ urea_nitrogen <dbl> 12.33, 3.00, 30.00, 18.00, 10.00, 5.00, 29.50, 29.00, 19…
## $ creatinine    <dbl> 0.57, 1.45, 0.85, 1.10, 1.00, 1.30, 0.65, 0.70, 1.20, 0.…
summary(polar_bears)
##     bear_id       capture_date            popn               sex           
##  Min.   :  697   Min.   :1983-04-23   Length:519         Length:519        
##  1st Qu.:20166   1st Qu.:2000-04-10   Class :character   Class :character  
##  Median :20705   Median :2008-04-12   Mode  :character   Mode  :character  
##  Mean   :18040   Mean   :2004-07-02                                        
##  3rd Qu.:21220   3rd Qu.:2011-03-31                                        
##  Max.   :99990   Max.   :2016-04-22                                        
##   age_class            mating            denning               lat       
##  Length:519         Length:519         Length:519         Min.   :62.46  
##  Class :character   Class :character   Class :character   1st Qu.:68.11  
##  Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Median :70.53  
##                                                           Mean   :69.86  
##                                                           3rd Qu.:71.00  
##                                                           Max.   :72.70  
##       long        urea_nitrogen     creatinine   
##  Min.   :-172.2   Min.   : 1.00   Min.   :0.350  
##  1st Qu.:-165.2   1st Qu.: 6.00   1st Qu.:0.850  
##  Median :-150.7   Median :11.00   Median :1.050  
##  Mean   :-153.7   Mean   :15.13   Mean   :1.079  
##  3rd Qu.:-146.5   3rd Qu.:21.25   3rd Qu.:1.285  
##  Max.   :-134.8   Max.   :69.00   Max.   :2.350
  1. (3 points) How are NA’s represented and where are they distributed in the variables? Use the naniar package to visualize the distribution of NA’s in the data and replace any alternative representations of NA with actual NA values.
polar_bears <- polar_bears %>% 
  replace_with_na(replace=list(mating="unknown")) %>% 
  replace_with_na(replace=list(denning="unknown"))
#polar_bears <- polar_bears %>% 
  #replace_with_na_all(condition = ~.x%in% c(common_na_strings,common_na_numbers))

This does not work in this situation

miss_var_summary(polar_bears)
## # A tibble: 11 × 3
##    variable      n_miss pct_miss
##    <chr>          <int>    <num>
##  1 mating           299     57.6
##  2 denning           91     17.5
##  3 bear_id            0      0  
##  4 capture_date       0      0  
##  5 popn               0      0  
##  6 sex                0      0  
##  7 age_class          0      0  
##  8 lat                0      0  
##  9 long               0      0  
## 10 urea_nitrogen      0      0  
## 11 creatinine         0      0
gg_miss_var(polar_bears)

  1. (6 points) Is sampling even for both sexes? Make a count the shows the number of males and females sampled by population and make a plot to visualize the counts.
polar_bears %>% 
  count(popn,sex) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x=popn,y=n,fill=sex))+
  geom_col(position="dodge")+
  labs(title="Male VS. Female distribution",x="Population Group",y="Count")+
  theme_minimal()

  1. (6 points) How many bears were sampled in each year for both populations? Make a line plot(s) to visualize sampling by year for both populations. (hint: you will need to pull apart the date to get the year in its own column)
polar_bears_4 <- 
  polar_bears %>% 
  separate(col=capture_date,
           into=c("year","month","day"),
           sep="-")
polar_bears_4 %>% 
  count(year,popn) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x=year,y=n,color=popn,group=popn))+
  geom_line()+
  geom_point()

polar_bears_4 %>% 
  count(year,popn) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x=year,y=n,color=popn,group=popn))+
  geom_line()+
  geom_point()+
  theme_minimal()+
  labs(title="Annual Sampling Count by Population",
       x="Year",
       y="Number of Bears Sampled",
       color="Population")+
  theme(axis.text.x=element_text(angle=45,vjust=1,hjust=1))

  1. (2 points) What are the min, median, mean, and max values for urea_nitrogen and creatinine?
polar_bears %>% 
  summarize(min_urea_nitrogen=min(urea_nitrogen,na.rm=T),
            max_urea_nitrogen=max(urea_nitrogen,na.rm=T),
            mean_urea_nitrogen=mean(urea_nitrogen,na.rm=T))
## # A tibble: 1 × 3
##   min_urea_nitrogen max_urea_nitrogen mean_urea_nitrogen
##               <dbl>             <dbl>              <dbl>
## 1                 1                69               15.1
polar_bears %>% 
  summarize(min_creatinine=min(creatinine,na.rm=T),
            max_creatinine=max(creatinine,na.rm=T),
            mean_creatinine=mean(creatinine,na.rm=T))
## # A tibble: 1 × 3
##   min_creatinine max_creatinine mean_creatinine
##            <dbl>          <dbl>           <dbl>
## 1           0.35           2.35            1.08
  1. (3 points) As explained above, the ratio of urea_nitrogen to creatinine is used as an index of fasting. To calculate the ratio, we need to multiply urea_nitrogen by 0.466, then divide by creatinine. Create a new variable called un_creat_ratio that contains this calculation.
polar_bears_6 <- 
  polar_bears %>% 
  mutate(un_creat_ratio=urea_nitrogen*0.466/creatinine)
  1. (4 points) The authors of the study categorized bears as fasting if their un_creat_ratio was less than or equal to 10. Use case_when() to create a new variable called fasting that categorizes bears as fasting or not.
polar_bears_6 <- 
  polar_bears_6 %>% 
  mutate(fasting=case_when(un_creat_ratio<=10~"Yes",
                           un_creat_ratio>10~"No"))
  1. (6 points) The authors found that the number of fasting bears increased in the SB population but decreased in the CS population. Make a plot(s) that shows the number of fasting bears by year for both populations to visualize this trend.
polar_bears_6 <- 
  polar_bears_6 %>% 
  separate(col=capture_date,
           into=c("year","month","day"),
           sep="-") 
polar_bears_6 %>% 
  filter(fasting=="Yes") %>% 
  count(year,popn) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x=year,y=n,color=popn,group=popn))+
  geom_line()+
  geom_point()+
  theme_minimal()+
  theme(axis.text.x=element_text(angle=45,hjust=1))+
  labs(title="Annual Trend of Fasting Bears by Population",
       x="Year",
       y="Number of Fasting Bears",
       color="Population")

  1. (8 points) Where are the polar bears used in this study located? Make a static (not leaflet) distribution map that shows the locations of bears by population. (hint: use color=popn in the aes() of geom_point())
summary(polar_bears)
##     bear_id       capture_date            popn               sex           
##  Min.   :  697   Min.   :1983-04-23   Length:519         Length:519        
##  1st Qu.:20166   1st Qu.:2000-04-10   Class :character   Class :character  
##  Median :20705   Median :2008-04-12   Mode  :character   Mode  :character  
##  Mean   :18040   Mean   :2004-07-02                                        
##  3rd Qu.:21220   3rd Qu.:2011-03-31                                        
##  Max.   :99990   Max.   :2016-04-22                                        
##   age_class            mating            denning               lat       
##  Length:519         Length:519         Length:519         Min.   :62.46  
##  Class :character   Class :character   Class :character   1st Qu.:68.11  
##  Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Median :70.53  
##                                                           Mean   :69.86  
##                                                           3rd Qu.:71.00  
##                                                           Max.   :72.70  
##       long        urea_nitrogen     creatinine   
##  Min.   :-172.2   Min.   : 1.00   Min.   :0.350  
##  1st Qu.:-165.2   1st Qu.: 6.00   1st Qu.:0.850  
##  Median :-150.7   Median :11.00   Median :1.050  
##  Mean   :-153.7   Mean   :15.13   Mean   :1.079  
##  3rd Qu.:-146.5   3rd Qu.:21.25   3rd Qu.:1.285  
##  Max.   :-134.8   Max.   :69.00   Max.   :2.350
lat <- c(62.46,72.70)
long <- c(-172.2,-134.8)
bbox <- make_bbox(long,lat,f=0.03)
map <- get_stadiamap(bbox,maptype="stamen_terrain",zoom=7)
## ℹ © Stadia Maps © Stamen Design © OpenMapTiles © OpenStreetMap contributors.
## ℹ 165 tiles needed, this may take a while (try a smaller zoom?)
ggmap(map)+
  geom_point(data=polar_bears,aes(long,lat,color=popn),size=0.75)

  1. (10 points) Build a simple shiny app that shows the range of un_creat_ratio values for both populations. The user should be able to select the fill variable and choose between sex, mating, age_class, denning, and fasting. Please use the code chunk below so that your exam knits.
names(polar_bears_6)
##  [1] "bear_id"        "year"           "month"          "day"           
##  [5] "popn"           "sex"            "age_class"      "mating"        
##  [9] "denning"        "lat"            "long"           "urea_nitrogen" 
## [13] "creatinine"     "un_creat_ratio" "fasting"
ui <- fluidPage(
  selectInput("y",
              "Select Your Y Variable",
              choices=c("sex","mating","age_class","denning","fasting"),
              selected="sex"),
  plotOutput("plot",width="500px",height="400px")
  
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {
  output$plot <- renderPlot({
    polar_bears_6 %>% 
      filter(mating!="NA") %>% 
      filter(denning!="NA") %>% 
      ggplot(aes(x=un_creat_ratio,
                 y=.data[[input$y]],
                 fill=popn))+
      geom_boxplot()+
      labs(title="the range of `un_creat_ratio` values for both populations")
  })
  
}

shinyApp(ui, server)

Submit the Midterm

  1. Save your work and knit the .rmd file.
  2. Open the .html file and “print” it to a .pdf file in Google Chrome (not Safari).
  3. Go to the class Canvas page and open Gradescope.
  4. Submit your .pdf file to the midterm assignment- be sure to assign the pages to the correct questions.
  5. Commit and push your work to your repository.
